title: Call stack and resource cost

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Call stack and resource cost; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Each recursive call adds a frame with parameters and locals until base cases return and frames unwind.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A depth-n linked-list recursion uses n frames even though each frame performs constant local work. Your goal is to visualize calls and convert a deeply linear recursion to iteration when appropriate. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Readiness, algorithms and analysis; Array representation and operations; Recursive problem design; Time, space and asymptotic growth. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Each recursive call adds a frame with parameters and locals until base cases return and frames unwind. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: C and C++ expose finite native call stacks without guaranteed tail-call elimination; Java likewise has bounded stack frames; Python has a comparatively low recursion limit, so all four implementations need an iterative alternative when depth scales with input.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Stack limits vary, tail-call optimization is not portable across the four languages, and hidden large depth can fail. The draft therefore rejects: Recursive algorithms use no extra space when they allocate no heap objects.

includes: Trace frames and relate recursion depth to auxiliary space and failure risk.
Visualize calls and convert a deeply linear recursion to iteration when appropriate.

C and C++ expose finite native call stacks without guaranteed tail-call elimination; Java likewise has bounded stack frames; Python has a comparatively low recursion limit, so all four implementations need an iterative alternative when depth scales with input.

walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A depth-n linked-list recursion uses n frames even though each frame performs constant local work.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Count call frames as auxiliary space; depth n recursion generally uses O(n) stack space.
